coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots inspired by structure-inherent targeting for nucleic acid imaging and localized photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ning
Du, Jianjun
Yao, Qichao
Ge, Haoying
Shi, Chao
Xu, Feng
Xian, Liman
Fan, Jiangli
Peng, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: Targeting capability is one of the most important properties of both imaging and therapeutic agents. The discovery of “structure-inherent targeting” (SIT) phenomena has benefitted the tailored design of fluorescent probes and photodynamic photosensitizers. Herein, SIT was combined with the inheritance of epigenetic characteristics from parent carbon sources to synthesize nucleic acid-targeted carbon dots (CDs) from quinoline derivatives, namely, 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs via a bottom-up strategy. These CDs exhibited RNA-selective fluorescence imaging, as confirmed using cell digestion, SYTO® RNASelect™ costaining, and nucleic acid titration. Furthermore, owing to iodine doping, I-CDs exhibited excellent PDT performance for killing cancer cells in vitro. Importantly, I-CDs could actively target and become enriched at tumor sites using folic acid-functionalized liposomes as tumor-homing vehicles, thus effectively inhibiting tumor growth in vivo. By screening the SIT carbon source, photodynamic CDs can provide a new platform for next-generation nucleic acid-targeting photodynamic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nucleic acid imaging
Structure-inherent targeting
Photodynamic therapy
Carbon dots
Carbon source–carbon dot inheritance strategy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of singlet oxygen quantum yield
Detection of ROS generation in cell
Structural characterization and spectral properties of CDs
Cell imaging and nuclear targeting mechanism of CDs
The singlet oxygen quantum yields and cytotoxicity experiments of CDs
Cell image and PDT experiment of I-CDs@FA in vitro and vivo

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21822804 , 22090011 , 21878039 and 21925802 ) and NSFC-Liaoning United Fund ( U1908202 ).

Jianjun Du is a Professor at the State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals at Dalian University of Technology. He received his B.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Applied Chemistry from Dalian University of Technology in 2004 and 2010, respectively. After his postdoctoral fellowship work at Nanyang Technological University (Singapore) in 2010–2013, he started his independent research career at Dalian University of Technology. His research is focused on organic/inorganic nanoparticles with functional organic molecules.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, high-resolution TEM images (right inset), and dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distributions (left inset) of (a) 1-CDs, (b) Cl-CDs, and (c) I-CDs. XPS spectra of (d) 1-CDs, (e) Cl-CDs, and (f) I-CDs. (g) High-resolution Cl 2p XPS spectrum of Cl-CDs. (h) High-resolution I 3d XPS spectrum of I-CDs. (i) UV–vis and fluorescence (FL) spectra of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs in ethanol.

(a) Colocation assays with Hoechst 33,342, SYTO® RNASelect™, and I-CDs incubated in live or fixed MCF-7 cells. (b) and (c) Normalized emission intensity distributions in the (I) cytoplasm, (II) nucleus, and (III) nucleolus (Hoechst 33342: blue line; SYTO® RNASelect™: green line, and I-CDs: red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Digestion experiments with I-CDs, Hoechst 33,342, and SYTO® RNASelect™. (b) Fluorescence spectra of I-CDs combined with ctDNA, yeast RNA, and ssDNA. (c) Fluorescence spectra of I-CDs, RNA-Cy5.5, and their complexes. (d) Fluorescence spectra of I-CDs, ssDNA-Cy5.5, and their complexes. (e) Schematic binding mechanism of CDs with nucleic acids.

Change in DPBF fluorescence with irradiation time in the presence of (a) 1-CDs, (b) Cl-CDs, and (c) I-CDs. (d) CLSM of ROS production in MCF-7 cells. (e) CLSM of Calcein-AM and PI stained MCF-7 cells with and without PDT treatment. (f) The survival rates of MCF-7 cells in presence of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs (0, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μg mL−1) in dark condition. (g) the survival rate of the MCF-7 cells after irradiation at 630 nm for 15 min.

(a) Preparation diagram of I-CDs@FA. (b) TEM image of I-CDs@FA (inset: DLS size distribution). (c) 3D deep fluorescence imaging of I-CDs and I-CDs@FA in a mixed culture of MCF-7 and Cos-7 cells. (d) Fluorescence imaging of I-CDs@FA in tumor-bearing Balb/c mice. (e) Fluorescence imaging of the main organs of the mice after dissection.

(a) Establishment of mouse PDT model. (b) Relative growth curves for 4T1 tumor-bearing mice in groups (I)–(Ⅵ). (c) Photograph of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice in groups (I)–(VI). (d) Photograph of bare tumors in groups (I)–(Ⅵ). (e) Tumor weights in groups (I)–(VI). (f) H&E-stained tumor sections for groups (I)–(Ⅵ).
Illustration of nucleic acid-targeting CDs for fluorescence imaging and PDT.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots inspired by structure-inherent targeting for nucleic acid imaging and localized photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, 2 Linggong Road, Dalian, 116024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, 2 Linggong Road, Dalian 116024, P.R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Dalian University of Technology, 26 Yucai Road, Jiangbei District, Ningbo, 315016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Dalian University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ningbo Institute of Dalian University of Technology, 26 Yucai Road, Jiangbei District, Ningbo, 315016, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, 2 Linggong Road, Dalian, 116024, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Targeting capability is one of the most important properties of both imaging and therapeutic agents. The discovery of “structure-inherent targeting” (SIT) phenomena has benefitted the tailored design of fluorescent probes and photodynamic photosensitizers. Herein, SIT was combined with the inheritance of epigenetic characteristics from parent carbon sources to synthesize nucleic acid-targeted carbon dots (CDs) from quinoline derivatives, namely, 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs via a bottom-up strategy. These CDs exhibited RNA-selective fluorescence imaging, as confirmed using cell digestion, SYTO® RNASelect™ costaining, and nucleic acid titration. Furthermore, owing to iodine doping, I-CDs exhibited excellent PDT performance for killing cancer cells in vitro. Importantly, I-CDs could actively target and become enriched at tumor sites using folic acid-functionalized liposomes as tumor-homing vehicles, thus effectively inhibiting tumor growth in vivo. By screening the SIT carbon source, photodynamic CDs can provide a new platform for next-generation nucleic acid-targeting photodynamic therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which have received substantial attention owing to their excellent fluorescence behavior, biocompatibility, stability, and ease of preparation, are considered excellent candidates in the fields of photocatalysis, bioimaging, sensing, and nanomedicine [1–7]. Regulating the structures, properties, and functions of CDs is considered fundamental for practical applications, but such control is not easy to realize. Over the past decade, carbon sources have been identified as a key factor for tuning the performance of CDs because some functional groups and properties of parent carbon sources can be inherited by CDs via a bottom-up synthesis strategy, namely, a carbon source–CDs inheritance strategy [8–11]. For example, when glutathione was used as a carbon source, near-infrared (NIR)-emissive CDs were obtained with a quantum yield of 16.8% because of sulfur and nitrogen doping, which not only allowed deep-tissue penetration but also avoided interference from spontaneous fluorescence in organisms [12]. Furthermore, CDs have been developed as promising carbon-based phototherapeutic agents by choosing appropriate carbon sources. For example, Zheng et al. used a hydrophobic cyanine dye (Cy) to synthesize CyCD, which successfully retained the NIR absorption and photothermal therapy properties of Cy [13]. Wang et al. prepared unique Mn-CDs from manganese (II) phthalocyanine, wherein manganese (II) catalyzed H2O2 to generate oxygen and thus promote photodynamic therapy (PDT) in hypoxic microenvironments [14]. However, the lifetime and propagation distance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) were limited, resulting in easy inactivation. Therefore, to realize precise imaging and theranostic applications, it is necessary to develop CDs with selective recognition and/or accurate targeting capabilities.

Synthetic engineering with various targeting groups (e.g., oligopeptides, antigens, and folic acid (FA)) is a conventional strategy for producing small-molecule-based probes and photosensitizers [15,16]. Recently, however, a novel “structure-inherent targeting” (SIT) concept, in which the inherent chemical structures of compounds provide specific targeting of a tissue of interest, has been developed and introduced successfully for the design of targeted imaging and treatment [17–21]. For example, in our previous report, SIT photosensitizers exhibited enhanced cancer ablation owing to their spontaneous organelle-targeting capability [22]. In addition, Kim et al. prepared SIT-based NIR bilayer nanovesicles for novel photoacoustic image-guided chemo-thermotherapy [23]. To date, various CDs have been developed from thousands of carbon sources, such as leaves, dyes, and chemicals [24–29]. Moreover, heteroatoms, functional groups, spectral properties, and even the PDT capability of carbon sources have been shown to be maintained in the corresponding CDs [30–32]. Therefore, we hypothesized that the SIT properties of carbon sources could also be successfully preserved in CDs.

As a proof of concept, quinoline derivatives were selected as SIT carbon sources for constructing nucleic acid-targeting CDs because most nucleic acid probes contained quinoline moieties for effective groove binding, electrostatic interactions, and π–π interactions [33–35]. Herein, 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal treatment process using quinoline derivatives as carbon sources (Scheme 1 ). All prepared CDs exhibited NIR absorption and emission because of nitrogen doping. 1-CDs, Cl-CDs and I-CDs demonstrated RNA-targeting imaging not only in cell digestion experiments but also in counterstaining (with SYTO® RNASelect™) and nucleic acid titration experiments. Furthermore, I-CDs exhibited an improved photosensitizing capability because of the heavy atom effect of iodine. With the aid of FA-modified lipidosomes (FA-DSPE-mPEG2000), I-CDs were actively delivered to tumor cells and efficiently killed cancer cells in vivo.

Methylene blue (MB), MTT, DPBF were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. 4-Methylquinoline and iodoethane were purchased from Energy Chemical. PBS and Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) were obtained Gibco. Reactive oxygen species detection kit DCFH-DA (2,7- dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate) and Annexin Calcein-AM and PI apoptosis kits were purchased from Nanjing KeyGen Biotech. Co. Ltd. (China). 5′-ACACTGATGCACTTTGTCTTCC-3′-Cy5.5, ssDNA: 5′-TATGCCTGCGAGCATTCAC-3′, 5′-ACACUGAUGCACUUUGUCUUCCCAG-3′-Cy5.5, RNA-1: 5′-UUGUACUACAAAAGUACUG-3′, RNA-2: 5′-ACGGCCGGCUGCCGAGCGCUACGGC-3′, RNA-3: 5′-AUACGAUACGUU AACAUAGCUACUA-3′, ssDNA-1: 5′-ACGTTCGAGATCATGCCTGCATGCA-3′ and RNA-4: 5′-GCAUGAUCUCGAACGUAUCCGAGCA-3′were purchased from Sangon Biotech. Co. Ltd. (China). The RNase A and DNase I kit were purchased from Thermofisher (Invitrogen). Fluorescence and UV–vis spectra were performed on Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (serial no. MY15210003) and Agilent Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer G6860A (serial no. MY1523004), respectively. The ultrapure water was acquired from Milli-Q systems throughout the experiments. TEM was obtained on Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV and 120 kV Transmission Electron Microscope (HT7700 EXALENS). XPS were obtained with an ESCALAB250Xi. MTT assay was measured on a Thermo Fisher Spectrophotometer 3020. The Olympus FV3000 confocal microscope imaging was used for cell imaging.

MNQ-I (20 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL anhydrous ethanol, sonicated for 2 min, and then transferred to a teflon autoclave. The hydrothermal reactor was placed in a vacuum drying oven, heated at 200 ℃ for 10 h. The mixed solution was centrifuged in centrifuge at 10000 r min−1 for 15 min to remove larger particles, then the supernatant liquid was filtered by 0.22 μm filter to obtain grass green liquid. Repeat the same operation twice. Finally, I-CDs was dried in a freeze dryer to obtain dark green powder. Meanwhile, other series of quinoline derivatives CDs were prepared by the same method as above using MNQ-Cl and 4-methylquinoline as carbon sources, respectively.

30 mg I-CDs, 120 mg Lecithin from Soybean, 10 mg cholesterol and 20 mg FA-DSPE-mPEG2000 with a quality ratio of 3:12:1:2 was dissolved in chloroform and stirred overnight. The chloroform solvent was then removed through rotary evaporation and placed in a vacuum drying box overnight. 20 mL ultrapure water was added and mixed with ultrasonic treatment, then frozen at -20 ℃ for 12 h. Secondly, the liquid was extruded through a high-pressure homogenizer then through 0.45 μm and 0.22 μm filter for 20 times to obtain the appropriate size. Finally, the liquid was dialyzed in 3500 molecular weight dialysis bags for 12 h to obtain I-CDs@FA solution, which was freeze-dried to obtain I-CDs@FA solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of singlet oxygen quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The singlet oxygen quantum yield was determined in paper using MB as the reference and DPBF as singlet oxygen trap. Firstly, the appropriate concentration of DPBF was added to 3 mL DCM to adjust fluorescence intensity at 457 nm. Then adding appropriate concentration of MB or I-CDs respectively to make sure that the mixture solutions have suitable absorbance at 630 nm. The mixed solution containing I-CDs was irradiated under 630 nm light for 0–150 s to test DPBF fluorescence curve. The mixed solution containing MB were exposed to 660 nm light irradiation and fluorescence spectrum was recorded. The 1O2 yield was calculated by following equation:
Φ △=Φ MB (kPs × FMB) / (kMB × FPs)×100 %
where Φ △ is singlet oxygen quantum yield of CDs. Φ MB represents singlet oxygen quantum yield of MB, is 0.57 in DCM. k represents decreasing slope of DPBF fluorescence intensity with the increase of irradiation time at 457 nm, and F is the correction factor (F = 1−10−
OD ), where OD is the UV absorption value of the respective photosensitizer at 630 or 660 nm. At the same time, the singlet oxygen quantum yield of 1-CDs and Cl-CDs (illumination time: 0–180 s) were tested by the above method, respectively.

The approximate concentration of 1 × 105 cells was seeded in petri dish and then incubated in incubator at 37 °C for 24 h (5% CO2 and 95% Air). The MCF-7 and 4T1 cells were incubated with 25 μg mL−1 CDs (1-CDs, Cl-CDs and I-CDs) and 2.5 μg mL−1 Hoechst 33342 for 30 min in incubator, respectively, then washed with PBS twice before imaging. Finally, the fluorescence images were captured under an Olympus FV-3000 after CDs excited by 640 nm laser. The blue channel signals at 430–470 nm and red channel signals at 650–750 nm was collected by 60 × objective lens, respectively. At the same time, the cell image of I-CDs@FA (incubation time: 60 min) were tested by the above method, respectively.

The cells were cultured under the same way as above. Iced ethanol was added to petri dish, placed in the refrigerator at -20 ℃ for 20 min to fixed cells, then removed iced ethanol and washed twice with PBS. The RNase A and DNase I were added to petri dish at 37 °C for 3 h to digest DNA or RNA, respectively. The dishes were divided into three groups: Control group, DNase I group and RNase A group. Then added I-CDs (1-CDs, Cl-CDs), Hoechst 33342 and SYTO® RNA Select™ and incubated for 30 min. Finally, the pre-treated cells were washed twice with PBS, then the fluorescence images were captured on Olympus FV-3000. The blue channel was excited at 405 nm and the fluorescence signal at 430–470 nm was collected by 60 × objective lens. The green channel was excited at 488 nm and the fluorescence signal at 500–600 nm was collected. The red channel was excited at 640 nm and the fluorescence signal at 650–750 nm was collected.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by reducing MTT to formazan crystals with mitochondrial dehydrogenases. Firstly, MCF-7 cells were seeded into each well of 96-well microtiter plate (Nunc, Denmark) with a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL in 100 μL DMEM containing 10% FBS and then incubated in incubator at 37 ℃ for 24 h. Secondly, after washed with PBS twice, the cells were treated with different concentrations of I-CDs (0, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 25 μg mL−1), then continue to incubate in incubator. After 30 min incubation in incubator, the plate was placed at 630 nm and 700 nm LED source with an average optical density of 20 mW cm-2 and irradiated for 15 min, respectively, then transfer to the incubator for 12 h. Thirdly, after washed twice with PBS, 100 μL MTT (0.5 mg mL−1) solution was added to each well in 96-well microtiter plate, then the cells were continuously cultured for 4 h in an incubator. Finally, after the MTT solution was carefully removed, 100 μL dimethyl sulfoxide was added to dissolve the formazan crystals in the plate. This plate was placed on a microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific) to measure the absorbance at 490 nm. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
Cell viability (%) = (ODCDs - ODKCDs) / (ODcontrol - ODKcontrol) × 100
where ODCDs is absorbance at different concentration of I-CDs, ODKCDs is absorbance of blank hole, ODcontrol is absorbance of control hole without treatment, and ODKcontrol is absorbance of only DMSO.
At the same time, the cell cytotoxicity of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs and I-CDs@FA were tested by the above method, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ROS generation in cell
ce:section.ce:para: The cells were cultured under the same way as above. The petri dish was divided into three groups: Containing 5 μM DCFH-DA, 25 μg mL−1 I-CDs +5 μM DCFH-DA and 25 μg mL−1 I-CDs +5 μM DCFH-DA + NaN3. Then, the cells were washed twice with PBS. Finally, the fluorescence images were captured on Olympus FV-3000 after I-CDs was irradiated by 640 nm laser for 1 min, and the fluorescence channel signals at 500–600 nm was collected by 60 × objective lens.

The cells were cultured under the same way. Then the dishes were exposed to the different following treatments: Control (CDs without light); CDs +630 nm LED source irradiated (20 mW cm−2 for 15 min). After 12 h incubation in incubator, the cells were further stained with Calcein-AM/PI Double Stain Kit according to the instruction manual. Finally, the fluorescence images were captured on Olympus FV-3000. The green channel (AM) was excited at 488 nm and the fluorescence signal at 500–600 nm was collected by 4 × objective lens. The red channel (PI) was excited at 561 nm and the fluorescence signal at 590–640 nm was collected.

The Balb/c mice (4–6 weeks old) were purchased from the SPF Experimental Animal Center at Dalian Medical University. The study was conducted in accordance with the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals published by the United States National Institutes of Health (8th edition, 2011), and the animal protocol was approved by the review board of the Animal Ethics Committee of Dalian University of Technology. The bioimaging experiments used the NightOWL II LB983 small animal imaging system equipped with a sensitive Charge Coupled Device camera, with the excitation at 665 nm and the 700 nm emission filter. The tumor model of Balb/c female mice was established by injecting 4T1 cells into the right axilla. The tumor volume needs to be increased to about 100 mm3 before PDT. In order to study targeting ability of I-CDs@FA, the 4T1 tumors-bearing Balb/c mice were injected by tail vein with 100 μL I-CDs@FA (5 mg kg−1 body weight), and the fluorescence signals were observed at different times (0, 4, 8, 20, 72, 96 h) after injected by NightOWL II LB983 small animal imaging system.

To study the antitumor efficiency of I-CDs@FA, all tumor mice were divided into six groups (N = 3): (I) PBS; (II) PBS + light; (III) I-CDs (dose: 5 mg kg−1); (IV) I-CDs + light; (Ⅴ) I-CDs@FA; (Ⅵ) I-CDs@FA + light. After 24 h post-injection, (II), (IV) and (Ⅵ) groups irradiated with 700 nm LED at the power intensity of 70 mW cm-2 for 30 min. Then, the tumor size and body weight were recorded every two day. The Balb/c tumor-bearing mice were given the drug again on the seventh day, continued observation and recording every two day after irradiated. The tumor volume was calculated according to the following equation: V= a × b 2/2, wherein V represents the tumor volume of mice, a and b are the length and width of tumor, respectively. After 21 d, the mice were euthanized and the main organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney were collected for pathological and histological analysis by hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and spectral properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using a series of quinoline derivatives as the carbon sources, 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs were prepared in ethanol solution by a one-step solvothermal method (Scheme S1 and S2). As shown in Fig. 1 a–c, 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs are amorphous with average sizes of 4.1, 4.9, and 4.8 nm in aqueous media, respectively. The zeta potentials of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs are +3.1, +10.5, and +7.02 mV, respectively (Fig. S1). Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) demonstrated that 1-CDs are composed of carbon (284.6 eV), nitrogen (399.3 eV), and oxygen (531.8 eV) with relative atomic percentages of 83.62%, 2.77%, and 13.61%, respectively (Fig. 1d). The high-resolution C1s spectrum showed that the CDs core consisted of CC/CC (284.4 eV) and CN/COH (285.1 eV) (Fig. S2a). The N1s spectrum revealed successful N-doping, with two main peaks corresponding to pyridinic N (399.0 eV) and graphitic N (399.9 eV), as shown in Fig. S2b [36]. The peaks at 531.6 eV (COC/COH) and 532.5 eV (CO) in O1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) confirmed the formation of oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g. COOH). Cl-CDs and I-CDs were found to contain 0.51% Cl (Fig. 1e and g) and 0.12% I (Fig. 1f and h), respectively, which directly confirmed heteroatom doping, whereas their high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra were similar to those of 1-CDs (Fig. S3 and S4). The UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs are shown in Fig. 1i. In ethanol, 1-CDs and Cl-CDs showed similar characteristic absorption peaks at 610 nm, whereas I-CDs exhibited two main absorption peaks at 630 and 700 nm, which were redshifted by the introduction of iodine (Fig. S5). The fluorescence emission wavelengths of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs were 635, 650, and 666 nm, respectively, when excited at 600 nm in ethanol solution. And the fluorescence lifetime of three CDs has been tested in Fig. S6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and nuclear targeting mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer cells (MCF-7 and 4T1) were used to investigate the biological imaging abilities of the CDs. 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs were added to MCF-7 and 4T1 cell dishes and incubated for 30 min. The strong red fluorescence signals of the CDs were mainly observed in the cytoplasm and nucleolus under confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), indicating good biocompatibility and penetrability (Fig. S7). It is well known that RNA exists in both the cytoplasm and nucleolus, whereas most DNA is located in the nucleus. The CDs were co-located with Hoechst 33342 and SYTO® RNASelect™ (a commercial nuclear dye) in living and fixed cells. The fluorescence images and normalized emission intensity distributions are shown in Figs. 2 a–c and S8. Compared with the strong signal of Hoechst 33342 in the nucleus, the CDs fluorescence was very weak in the nucleus but strong in the cytoplasm and nucleolus. Under the same conditions, SYTO® RNASelect™ also showed high fluorescence intensity in the cytoplasm and nucleolus, consistent with the fluorescence signal of I-CDs (as an example) in live cells. In contrast, in fixed cells, SYTO® RNASelect™ showed strong fluorescence signals in both the nucleus and nucleolus, whereas I-CDs only emitted fluorescence in the cytoplasm and nucleolus, indicating an ability to distinguish between RNA and DNA [37].

To further confirm the binding ability of the CDs with RNA, MCF-7 cells were used in digestion experiments with ribonuclease (RNase A) and deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I) [38]. After RNase A and DNase I were added to digest nucleic acids in fixed MCF-7 cells, the cells were stained with CDs, Hoechst 33,342, and SYTO® RNASelect™. As shown in Fig. 3 a, in the absence of DNase I and RNase A, red fluorescence (from I-CDs) was observed in the cytoplasm and nucleolus, green fluorescence (from SYTO® RNASelect™) was observed in the nucleolus, and blue fluorescence (from Hoechst 33,342) was observed in the nucleus of the MCF-7 cells. When the fixed MCF-7 cells were treated with RNase A, the red and green fluorescence almost disappeared in the nucleolus, whereas the blue fluorescence from Hoechst 33342 was unaffected. In contrast, only the blue fluorescence disappeared when the cells were treated with DNase I. The nucleic acid digestion experiments confirmed that CDs preferred to bind RNA rather than DNA in vitro, and this SIT capability was derived from quinoline derivative carbon source (Fig. S9 and S10).

To explore the nuclear targeting mechanism, ctDNA, ssDNA, and RNA were added to the CDs aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 3b, the addition of ctDNA leaded to a weak emission wavelength red-shift and fluorescence intensity decreases of I-CDs, while the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs decreased rapidly after adding different types of RNA and ssDNA (yeast RNA, ssDNA, T-RNA, DNA of salmon testes, RNA-1, RNA-2, RNA-3, RNA-4, ssDNA-1). However, the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs interacting with different types of RNA was basically the same, which could not be effectively distinguished in Fig. S11. The experiment results of the interaction between nucleic acid and I-CDs are similar to aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (Fig. S12). Because single-stranded nucleic acids are more flexible than double-stranded DNA, ssDNA and RNA are more likely to intertwine and aggregate with CDs than rigid dsDNA (Fig. 3e). Moreover, Cy5.5-modified ssDNA and Cy5.5-modified RNA were added to interact with CDs, both of which were quenched rapidly by our CDs via the Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism (Figs. 3c, d, and S13). According to the above results, the quinoline derivatives-based CDs could selectively bind nucleic acids, especially RNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The singlet oxygen quantum yields and cytotoxicity experiments of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cell imaging experiments indicated that the CDs accumulated in the nucleolus by interacting with RNA, which is important for improving PDT by producing ROS in the key location of the nucleus. Inspired by heavy atomic effects in organic photosensitizers, iodine atoms were introduced into the CDs in this work [39,40]. The singlet oxygen (1O2) quantum yields of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs were evaluated using 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a 1O2 capture agent. The oxidation of DPBF to 1, 2-dibenzoylbenzene results in an obvious fluorescence decrease at 458 nm [36]. As shown in Figs. 4 a–c and S14–S18, the addition of I-CDs resulted in a greater decrease in the fluorescence intensity of DPBF at 457 nm than the addition of 1-CDs or Cl-CDs. The 1O2 quantum yields of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs were calculated to be 2.0%, 1.70%, and 2.9%, respectively, which is consistent with the above results. To demonstrate that I-CDs could produce 1O2 in the cell microenvironment, we used 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetates (DCFH-DA) as an intracellular indicator that produces green fluorescence via 1O2 oxidation. As shown in Fig. 4d, the MCF-7 cells incubated with DCFH-DA alone generated weak green fluorescence after 640 nm laser irradiation. However, strong green fluorescence was generated when I-CDs and DCFH-DA were added simultaneously to the MCF-7 cells. However, this signal disappeared when NaN3 was introduced as a reactive oxygen scavenger. The photodynamic effect of I-CDs was further confirmed by Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) staining experiments (Figs. 4e and S19).

To investigate the phototoxicity of the CDs in detail, the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was used as a standard method to determine relative cell viability [41]. First, MCF-7 cells were treated with different concentrations of 1-CDs, Cl-CDs, and I-CDs (0–25 μg mL−1). As shown in Fig. 4d, the cell survival rate was more than 85% after 12 h incubation under dark conditions, indicating that none of the CDs showed obvious cytotoxicity. However, following irradiation with a 630 nm LED (20 mW cm-2, 15 min), the survival rates of the MCF-7 cells gradually decreased with increasing CDs concentration. When the concentration of I-CDs was 25 μg mL−1, the cell survival rate was less than 20%, which is considerably lower than those in the presence of 1-CDs and Cl-CDs under the same conditions (Fig. 4e). Furthermore, the MTT results with 4T1 cells lines confirmed that the introduction of iodine can improve PDT without increasing the dark toxicity of the CDs (Fig. S20).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell image and PDT experiment of I-CDs@FA in vitro and vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Although our CDs exhibited organelle-targeting capabilities, they could not distinguish tumor cells from normal cells. As a delivery system folic acid-modified liposomes (FA-DSPE-mPEG2000) not only passively enhanced penetration effects but also actively targeted overexpressed folate receptors on tumor cell membrane to achieve accumulation in tumor. Moreover, the mPEG framework of FA-DSPE-mPEG2000 phospholipids effectively avoids liposomes recognized and phagocytized by the reticuloendothelial system [42]. I-CDs@FA was produced from soybean lecithin, cholesterol, FA-DSPE-mPEG2000, and I-CDs by high-pressure homogenizer extrusion with 33% encapsulation efficiency (Figs. 5 a and S21). As shown in Fig. 5b, the average size of I-CDs@FA was approximately 96.5 nm, and the zeta potential changed from +7.02 mV for I-CDs to -4.15 mV for I-CDs@FA (Fig. S22 and S23). I-CDs@FA exhibited good fluorescence imaging behavior in both MCF-7 and 4T1 cells (Fig. S24). Moreover, a coincubation model involving both MCF-7 and Cos-7 cells was used to evaluate the targeting ability of I-CDs@FA. Because MCF-7 cells highly expressed folic acid receptors on their surface, I-CDs@FA exhibited selectivity for the MCF-7 cells, as expected, whereas I-CDs stained both the MCF-7 and Cos-7 cells without distinction (Fig. 5c). Balb/c tumor-bearing mice were selected as animal models for further evaluation in vivo. The Balb/c tumor-bearing mice were imaged using a NightOWL II LB983 animal imaging system after injection with I-CDs@FA in the tail vein. As shown in Fig. 5d, I-CDs@FA gradually accumulated in the tumor sites, reaching a maximum level after ∼20 h. Importantly, the fluorescence signal remained stable for approximately 72 h, indicating that I-CDs@FA has a long retention time in tumors. The tumors and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were collected from the Balb/c tumor-bearing mice after 4 days to study the distribution of residual CDs. In addition to tumors, the accumulation of CDs was mainly observed in the liver and kidney, indicating that the CDs were effectively metabolized (Fig. 5e).

The results of the in vitro experiments (MTT assay, apoptosis, and Calcein-AM and PI imaging) confirmed that I-CDs@FA and I-CDs exhibited comparable photodynamic effects (Fig. S25–S27). Furthermore, the feasibility of using I-CDs@FA for antitumor therapy was investigated by dividing tumor-bearing mice into six groups to assess the PDT effect over 21 days: (Ⅰ) PBS, (Ⅱ) PBS + light (700 nm, 70 mW cm−2, 30 min), (Ⅲ) I-CDs (dose: 5 mg kg-1), (Ⅳ) I-CDs + light, (Ⅴ) I-CDs@FA, and (Ⅵ) I-CDs@FA + light. The range of 600–950 nm is considered the optimal “therapeutic window;” therefore, excitation at 700 nm was used in the PDT test because I-CDs@FA has a broad absorption band at 500–700 nm (Fig. S28). Based on the fluorescence imaging results in Fig. 6a, 20 h post-injection was selected as the optime time point for PDT. The tumor volumes and body weights of the six groups were measured and recorded every 2 days, and no obvious weight loss was observed in any of the six groups over the entire treatment process (Fig. S29). As shown in Fig. 6 b, there was no significant tumor growth in the mice in group Ⅵ, suggesting that I-CDs@FA effectively inhibited tumor growth. By contrast, in the other groups, the tumor size increased significantly (more than 7-fold) over time, irrespective of irradiation. In addition, the tumor growth rate of the I-CDs treatment group was similar to that of the PBS group with/without light irradiation, indicating the importance of targeting. Finally, all the tumors and major organs were dissected after 21 days of treatment. The tumor volume and weight in group VI were significantly smaller than those in the other five groups, which is consistent with the above results (Fig. 6c–e). Furthermore, hematoxylin–eosin (H&E)-stained tumor sections showed that the tumor tissue in group Ⅵ was significantly damaged, whereas no obvious tumor necrosis was observed in the other five groups (Fig. 6f). Importantly, the H&E staining results of the major organs (heart, liver spleen, lung, and kidney) demonstrated little physiological morphological abnormalities or histopathological damage (Fig. S30). These experimental results confirmed that I-CDs@FA is a safe and effective photosensitizer for tumor inhibition.

In summary, a series of CDs were developed using quinoline derivatives as carbon sources. Based on the carbon source–CDs inheritance strategy, the CDs were anticipated to possess a SIT capability for the nucleus. Unexpectedly, the CDs exhibited a preference for flexible RNA and ssDNA rather than rigid dsDNA, as revealed by cell digestion and SYTO® RNASelect™ re-staining experiments. Furthermore, self-assembly with folic acid-functionalized liposomes allowed the CDs to target tumors, and I-CDs@FA exhibited excellent photodynamic effects for killing cancer cells, resulting in successful tumor suppression in vivo. The results of this work provide a valuable strategy for developing next-generation nucleic acid-targeting photosensitizers with great potential for clinical application in cancer treatment.

Ning Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing-original draft. Jianjun Du: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project adminis-tration, Funding acquisition, Writing-review & editing. Qichao Yao: Discussed results. Haoying Ge and Chao Shi: Investigation, Resources. Feng Xu and Liman Xian: Formal analysis, Methodology. Jiangli Fan: Supervision, Project adminis-tration, Funding acquisition. Xiaojun Peng: Supervision, Project adminis-tration, Funding acquisition

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21822804, 22090011, 21878039 and 21925802) and NSFC-Liaoning United Fund (U1908202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130322.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ning Xu received his B.E. degree from Anhui University of Science & Technology in 2015. Currently, he is pursuing his Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiaojun Peng at Dalian University of Technology. His current research interests include synthesis and applications of carbon dots.

Jianjun Du is a Professor at the State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals at Dalian University of Technology. He received his B.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Applied Chemistry from Dalian University of Technology in 2004 and 2010, respectively. After his postdoctoral fellowship work at Nanyang Technological University (Singapore) in 2010–2013, he started his independent research career at Dalian University of Technology. His research is focused on organic/inorganic nanoparticles with functional organic molecules.

Xiaojun Peng received his Ph.D. in 1990 at Dalian University of Technology. After completing postdoctoral research at Nankai University (China), he has worked at the Dalian University of Technology since 1992. In 2001 and 2002 he was a visiting scholar at Stockholm University and Northwestern University (USA). Currently he is the director of the State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals at Dalian University of Technology. His research interests cover dyes for fluorescent bioimaging/labeling and digital printing/recording.
